author: @garrettmillard525
text:
I would probably stick it in the ocean so I can have super consistent power. What a nightmare that would be trying to get that highway electricity anywhere useful. And a nightmare for those running the grid dealing with random intermittent bursts of charge. Integration with the grid demands one most basic principle: forecastability. Must remember that the cost of the power generation itself is only a small fraction of the cost of delivering super reliable electric 24/7. 

Of course, as homes and institutions begin installing batteries to complement their solar, some low cost small scale wind harvesting could certainly see a use case! 

Moreover, I have a hard time seeing it last with such variable speeds. But I do still have hope that similar designs will be deployed to capture the infinite and vast quantities of energy in the waves. Would love to see some videos on this (much more than just tidal!).

author: @damontownsend8390
text: What about a magnet at the bottom of the “leaf”, and a coil at the base.  Each flutter will pass the magnet over the coil, and the mass will help with a “flywheel” effect.

author: @wiradharma7030
text:
I am interested with gearless magnetic transmission. There is a video that explain this. He use 3 disc. The first and third use 16 pole magnets, the middle disc consist of 10 metal inserts. In the first disc the magnets oriented in alternating pole, aka n - s - n - ... configuration. The third consist of 4 block of magnets pole, 4 magnets oriented in the  same direction followed by another 4 in reverse and so on. 16 poles in the first disc and 4 poles on the third.

The transmission have 4:1 ratio, and could work up to around 8500 rpm. I curious about this kind of transmission as there will always be wasted energy in transmission and higher pole motor also means higher loss to fighting the back EMF. Still too busy in real world😭

author: @dremaboy777
text: Good stuff.

author: @rayadagio
text: I like this idea represented! But will it be still simple and cheap if a generator unit is added?

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: I think so, you could simply use a tiny magnet at the bottom of the flap and a single piece of copper wire looping a few times below the magnet with 4 small diodes to rectify the AC output. The wire/coil and frame could be made out of a single PCB to completely automate manufacturing.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Perhaps one might combine the energy recovery and sensitivity of such devices to enhance performance of wind turbines in low wind conditions?  Maybe a hinged vane at the leading or trailing edge of a VAWT blade, working like a luff or a wing flap.

author: @MrFujack103
text: This is like I was thinking about in a boat or car you could use a pendulum to capture the motion. This as far as I can see wouldn’t use any energy itself so 100% output

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
If you capture energy from anything, it's using that thing's energy. If you capture it from wind, wind slows down. If you capture it from heat, something gets colder. If you capture it from light, less light is reflected (it get's darker). You can only move energy from one place to another.
You could try to capture energy from unwanted motion of the boat (side to side), damping the rocking motion. But as you also see in that sentence a source of energy (rocking motion) and how you change a source if you capture that energy (damping the rocking motion). So directly you would take energy from water waves - wave will get a tiny bit smaller.

author: @totherarf
text:
As per usual, something to make you go ..... Hmmmmm!
If you are on a flutter device I think sticking a magnet and coil is the way to go.  

But I think the important question is how you network them all to get a useable output.
I can just see a picture in my head of the rolling moreland covered with these in swaths spaced every foot or so. You would be talking of 100's of thousands ....... but there is a Lot of empty mores out there not used for much else! ;o)

author: @salimufari
text: My only issue with this design is the lifespan. A few thousand cycles & that hinge is toast I would imagine.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
The common Door Hinge is designed and tested to get about 2 and an arf million cycles in its lifespan.
Spings do tremendously better.
The flutterer he is showing has a plastic hinge, whis is sel-lubricating.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 - live hinges... Worse performance than a spring (as it tends to deform plastically).
